raw_case:
A case of postgunshot deep femoral artery pseudo-aneurysm managed with coil embolization: A case report and literature review
In this case, the patient sustained a gunshot injury to the thigh and underwent surgery to treat a comminuted fracture. Six weeks after discharge, the patient presented with upper-thigh pain. Doppler ultrasound showed a deep femoral artery pseudoaneurysm, confirmed with computerized tomography (CT). We used coil embolization, a newly used method worldwide in recent years, to occlude the pseudoaneurysm. This reflects the complex, unknown mechanisms that contribute to such rare findings and draws attention to cases that require more extensive studies to understand potential pathological processes, leading to better management and improved outcomes. Furthermore, this case includes figures and images that illustrate the findings both pre- and postintervention, significantly enhancing the visual appeal of the procedure. Additionally, it provides a comprehensive review of other related cases, each with detailed presentations. Finally, this case illuminates the innovative application of embolization techniques in developing nations, which has resulted in lifesaving, cost-effectiveness, and a reduction in complications compared to other techniques.

Introduction

Deep femoral artery pseudoaneurysms are considered to be rare, with a known incidence of 2% of the peripheral artery [1]. The most frequent causes were thought to be thigh-penetrating trauma and iatrogenic injuries sustained during vascular and orthopedic procedures [2]. The presentation can be varied between asymptomatic patients, especially in the first forty-eight hours, so it is easy to be missed, or patients with mild symptoms such as swelling, claudication, thigh pain, and, in severe cases, hemorrhage and subsequent shock [3]. As a result, there are still debates and challenges about the best way to manage such a condition. However, the endovascular method has been novel in recent years. This report describes the treatment of a 29-year-old male patient who had a gunshot injury to his thigh that got complicated with active bleeding and hematoma. The patient also underwent proper therapy for a comminuted neck-femur fracture. But when the patient came back, the cause of his thigh pain was found to be a deep femoral artery pseudoaneurysm after further investigation. A nonprofit medical and academic institution admitted and managed the patient.This case sheds light on the presentation of deep femoral artery pseudoaneurysm, a condition that few cases and studies globally have reported. Locally in Palestine, it is believed to be the first case, as several cases have reported femoral artery pseudoaneurysm but not deep femoral artery. It also talks about the endovascular approach, which could be a great way to treat pseudoaneurysms of the deep femoral artery since there aren't any widely agreed upon methods for dealing with these cases in the literature.

Case presentation

The emergency department received a 29-year-old male patient in an ambulance, who had sustained a gunshot injury to his right thigh. The patient had stable vital signs (blood pressure 110/76, heart rate 97, O2 saturation 95, respiratory rate 17, temperature 36.9). The examination revealed the bullet's inlet in the anterior upper medial thigh, which had a small wound, and its outlet in the posterior upper thigh.Imaging revealed a comminuted neck-femur fracture. The patient was taken to the operating room, where bleeding control and hematoma evacuation were performed followed by closed reduction and fixation by a dynamic hip screw and plate for the fracture. Several days later, he was discharged on cefpodoxime 200 mg for 10 days, and Rivaroxaban 10 mg once daily with analgesia.Six weeks later, the patient experienced pain in the upper side of the thigh that gradually increased. So, he sought medical help at our hospital. On examination, he was hemodynamically stable (blood pressure of 130/80, heart rate of 95, O2 saturation of 98%, respiratory rate of 17, temperature of 37.2); there was no history of any kind of trauma after he had been discharged; he had a well-healed scar in the right upper thigh with intact peripheral pulsation bilaterally. A Doppler ultrasound of the lower limb revealed a 2.2 × 2 cm pseudoaneurysm sac with a very short neck that started from the deep femoral artery right after the common femoral artery (CFA) split into 2. A contrast-enhanced computerized tomography (CT) scan of the lower extremity showed a 2 × 2 cm pseudoaneurysm of the proximal profunda femoris artery (Fig. 1). After that, the patient was scheduled for pseudoaneurysm embolization.We punctured the left common femoral artery (CFA) under US guidance and sterile technique, then performed a crossover, selective right external iliac artery engagement, and balloon-assisted thrombin injection of the pseudoaneurysm. A trial to cross beyond the pseudoaneurysm was unsuccessful due to the dissection and fragile vessels. We used 2 balloons to protect the superficial femoral artery (SFA) and CFA. Next, we performed a US-guided thrombin injection Fig. 1), using approximately 2000 international units (IU). However, it did not cause pseudoaneurysm thrombosis. So, a decision about embolization coiling was taken using Progreat microcatheter and embolization coils (Fig. 2). The final angiogram showed around 95%-97% occlusion and patent SFA. Also, below knee vessels and some of the branches of the profunda femoris artery (Fig. 2).The day after the procedure, a doppler ultrasound confirmed the successful closure of the pseudoaneurysm. The patient was discharged on cefpodoxime 200 milligrams for 10 days. Seven weeks later, in a follow-up visit, the patient had no complaints, and a Doppler ultrasound showed no considerable defect. A summary of the case presentation is available in a time plan.

Discussion

Pseudoaneurysm usually develops after blood leaks from the arterial lumen because of disruption in layers of the arterial wall [4]. This results in a blood flow cavity in the outer wall of the artery or the nearby tissues [5]. Blood flow outside the normal layers of the arterial wall causes it to differ from a true aneurysm. The compressed surrounding tissues, not the artery wall, compose the wall of a false aneurysm.The diagnosis of deep femoral artery aneurysm is considered a challenge because of several factors. Firstly, this artery is located deep in the thigh, which makes the diagnosis far to be expected [3,6]. Secondly, this report's known late presentation and clinical symptoms like swelling and thigh pain [7] confirm the diagnosis. However, unexplained anemia and pulsatile mass would support the diagnosis [7]. In this case, ultrasonography was a very useful tool for ruling out conditions like deep vein thrombosis and wound infection.Indeed, the need for rapid diagnosis and consequently choosing the best treatment are crucial. There are several approaches to treating the pseudoaneurysms. The surgical approach is the oldest [8]. Surgery is needed when there is haematoma-related skin necrosis, neurological symptoms, compression of nearby structures, a possible local infection, other treatments not working, rapidly growing or very large pseudoaneurysms, or when there is bleeding, shock, or a retroperitoneal puncture site [9]. However, several noninvasive or minimally invasive approaches are used, such as ultrasound-guided compression therapy, endovascular stent-graft implantation, and injection of 0.9% saline solution alongside the pseudoaneurysm's neck and coil embolization. However, these approaches have become less common in recent years [10]. Simple pseudoaneurysms with a long, thin neck and moderate antiplatelet therapy should first attempt ultrasound-guided compression therapy, a well-established and noninvasive procedure. However, repeated and nonduplex guided compression poses a significant risk of deep venous thrombosis or skin maceration [9]. Stent implantation is recognized as a complex and inefficient procedure that does not enhance success rates, making it of minimal significance, particularly in the groin area [11]. Moreover, the placement of covered stents is not favorable in the area of femoral bifurcation because it's an area of flexion and the risk to occlude the deep femoral artery [12].The most recent approach was duplex ultrasound-guided percutaneous thrombin injection which is known to be safe and effective alternative to surgery and compression [9]. According to literature, the effectiveness of thrombin injection exceeded 90% which is high and considered an excellent success. Reports indicated that 11%-15% required additional thrombin injections for complete pseudoaneurysm obliteration [13]. Generally, vascular or allergic complications account for the 1.4%–4% reported rate of complications. While some studies do not include surgical revision as a complication, the rate is still between 2.8% and 7.4%, significantly lower than the reported rate of surgical approach, which is estimated to be around 33% [14]. The high rate of postoperative complications, most of which are associated with wound healing, could potentially explain this high rate. Furthermore, reports indicate a mortality rate ranging from 3.8% to 7.9% [15]. Patients receiving aggressive anticoagulant therapy or dual antiplatelet therapy, as well as those experiencing severe pain during compression, may benefit from thrombin injection [16]. On the other hand, coil embolization, known to be less invasive than conventional surgery, is advantageous in cases involving the mid-distal part of the deep femoral artery, as it allows for collateral sparing. However, this technique is complicated and expensive and that does not improve success rates and is therefore of only minor importance (in the groin region) [11].Duplex ultrasonography, which can provide information on the size, length of the cavity, and eventually the diameter of the neck [17], can be used in the diagnosis. CT angiography would be a favorable choice as it helps to give a clear image that reflects the anatomy and eventually the operative strategy [8]. From our point of view, CT angiography was necessary for preoperative planning and the decision to proceed with more invasive vascular procedures or interventional radiology.In choosing the treatment option, the length of the neck, size of the pseudoaneurysm and location (distal or proximal deep femoral artery), the presence or absence of collaterals originating from the pseudoaneurysm, the presence or absence of prior groin and the vascular access (ipsilateral or contralateral) should be considered in each case [8].Typically, surgeons treat proximal pseudoaneurysms surgically to maintain collaterals, and they recommend proximal ligation for those located distally [17]. However, peripheral artery disease may worsen due to late-presenting acute limb ischemia. Embolization is preferred in our case due to patient stability and less invasiveness.Few cases have reported deep femoral artery aneurysms after a traumatic event. Pecoraro et al. reported 8 cases with symptoms like swelling, pain, wound bleeding, and anemia. Furthermore, a case series [18] reported 4 cases in which severe pain and swelling, along with ecchymosis in the perineum or thigh, were the main symptoms. The supplementary file provides a literature review table.Advancements in interventional procedures reduce the need for vascular and invasive surgical interventions, but not all facilities have access to embolization. This study will help clinicians make informed decisions about patient management in challenging situations.

Conclusion

Deep femoral artery pseudo-aneurysm is a rare entity, and endovascular interventions provide an effective and less invasive approach in such stable cases.

Author contribution statement

The authors have participated equally in this case including data collection, reviewing the literature, and drafting the manuscript.

Patient consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication of this case report and any accompanying images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 29-year-old male patient.
- **Chief Complaint:** Pain in the upper right thigh six weeks after discharge from a gunshot wound treatment.
- **History of Present Illness:** Sustained a gunshot injury to the right thigh, resulting in a comminuted neck-femur fracture. Treatment involved bleeding control, hematoma evacuation, and fracture fixation with a dynamic hip screw and plate. The patient developed increasing pain in the upper thigh approximately six weeks post-discharge.
- **Past Medical History:** None reported.
- **Personal History:** None reported.
- **Family History:** None reported.
- **Physical Examination:** Hemodynamically stable with a well-healed scar on the right upper thigh. Peripheral pulsations intact bilaterally.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Doppler ultrasound: Identified a 2.2 × 2 cm pseudoaneurysm arising from the deep femoral artery.
  - Contrast-enhanced CT angiography: Confirmed the pseudoaneurysm measuring 2 × 2 cm at the proximal profunda femoris artery.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Possibilities:**
   - Given the patient's post-gunshot wound and fracture history, potential causes of thigh pain included:
     - Vascular complications such as pseudoaneurysm or thrombosis.
     - Orthopedic issues such as complications from fracture fixation or post-traumatic arthritis.
     - Infectious processes such as abscess or osteomyelitis.

2. **Focused Consideration of Diagnoses:**
   - **Pseudoaneurysm:** Strongly suspected due to imaging findings (pulsatile 2×2 cm sac with short neck) on Doppler and CT. Supported by the patient's history of trauma, delayed onset of symptoms, and absence of infection signs.
   - **Deep vein thrombosis (DVT):** Excluded due to lack of swelling, Doppler findings, and absence of suggestive history or risk factors (e.g., prolonged immobility).
   - **Infection (abscess, osteomyelitis):** Excluded based on physical and imaging findings (no fever, localized signs, or destructive bone changes).
   - **Fracture fixation-related complications:** Deemed less likely as pain was localized to the vascular region, and the fixation site showed no abnormalities on follow-up imaging.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: A pseudoaneurysm of the deep femoral artery, likely resulting from trauma related to the gunshot injury. Diagnosis was confirmed by Doppler ultrasound and CT imaging.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Analyze the Chief Complaint and History of Present Illness:** The patient, a 29-year-old male, presents with right upper thigh pain six weeks post-discharge after treatment for a gunshot wound. The gunshot wound caused a comminuted femur neck fracture, treated with bleeding control, hematoma evacuation, and dynamic hip screw and plate fixation. The delayed onset of pain post-discharge is a key factor to consider.

<step 2> **Consider the Mechanism of Injury and Potential Complications:** A gunshot wound is a high-energy trauma that can damage not only bone but also surrounding soft tissues, including blood vessels. Femoral fractures, especially those caused by gunshot wounds, are associated with a risk of vascular injury. Surgical intervention, while necessary, can also sometimes lead to vascular complications.  Post-surgical complications in this context can include infection, delayed union/non-union, nerve injury, and vascular injury.

<step 3> **Evaluate the Ancillary Test Results:** Doppler ultrasound and contrast-enhanced CT angiography both confirm the presence of a 2x2 cm pseudoaneurysm arising from the deep femoral artery. This is a critical finding. A pseudoaneurysm is a contained rupture of an artery wall, where blood leaks out and forms a sac outside the vessel wall, contained by surrounding tissues. This is a known complication of vascular trauma and surgical procedures.

<step 4> **Integrate Clinical Presentation with Test Results:** The patient's chief complaint of increasing thigh pain six weeks after discharge, in the context of a previous gunshot wound and femoral fracture repair, strongly correlates with the finding of a deep femoral artery pseudoaneurysm.  The pain is likely due to the pseudoaneurysm itself, possibly through expansion, pressure on surrounding tissues, or nerve irritation. The timing of pain onset is consistent with the potential development and expansion of a pseudoaneurysm post-trauma and surgery.

<step 5> **Formulate the Final Diagnosis:** Based on the patient's history of gunshot injury to the thigh with femoral fracture, subsequent surgical fixation, the delayed onset of thigh pain, and the definitive radiological evidence of a pseudoaneurysm of the deep femoral artery, the final diagnosis is directly linked to this vascular complication.

### Answer:
Pseudoaneurysm of the Deep Femoral Artery

The gunshot wound caused a comminuted femur neck fracture, treated with bleeding control, hematoma evacuation, and dynamic hip screw and plate fixation. The delayed onset of pain post-discharge is a key factor to consider.

**Consider the Mechanism of Injury and Potential Complications:** A gunshot wound is a high-energy trauma that can damage not only bone but also surrounding soft tissues, including blood vessels. Femoral fractures, especially those caused by gunshot wounds, are associated with a risk of vascular injury. Surgical intervention, while necessary, can also sometimes lead to vascular complications.  Post-surgical complications in this context can include infection, delayed union/non-union, nerve injury, and vascular injury.

**Evaluate the Ancillary Test Results:** Doppler ultrasound and contrast-enhanced CT angiography both confirm the presence of a 2x2 cm pseudoaneurysm arising from the deep femoral artery. This is a critical finding. A pseudoaneurysm is a contained rupture of an artery wall, where blood leaks out and forms a sac outside the vessel wall, contained by surrounding tissues. This is a known complication of vascular trauma and surgical procedures.

**Integrate Clinical Presentation with Test Results:** The patient's chief complaint of increasing thigh pain six weeks after discharge, in the context of a previous gunshot wound and femoral fracture repair, strongly correlates with the finding of a deep femoral artery pseudoaneurysm.  The pain is likely due to the pseudoaneurysm itself, possibly through expansion, pressure on surrounding tissues, or nerve irritation. The timing of pain onset is consistent with the potential development and expansion of a pseudoaneurysm post-trauma and surgery.

**Formulate the Final Diagnosis:** Based on the patient's history of gunshot injury to the thigh with femoral fracture, subsequent surgical fixation, the delayed onset of thigh pain, and the definitive radiological evidence of a pseudoaneurysm of the deep femoral artery, the final diagnosis is directly linked to this vascular complication.

Step 2 Pseudoaneurysm was strongly suspected due to imaging findings (pulsatile 2×2 cm sac with short neck) on Doppler and CT, supported by the patient's history of trauma, delayed onset of symptoms, and absence of infection signs.
Step 3 Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) was excluded due to lack of swelling, Doppler findings, and absence of suggestive history or risk factors (e.g., prolonged immobility).
Step 4 Infection (abscess, osteomyelitis) was excluded based on physical and imaging findings (no fever, localized signs, or destructive bone changes).
Step 5 Fracture fixation-related complications were deemed less likely as pain was localized to the vascular region, and the fixation site showed no abnormalities on follow-up imaging.
Step 6 Final diagnosis: A pseudoaneurysm of the deep femoral artery, likely resulting from trauma related to the gunshot injury.
Step 7 Diagnosis was confirmed by Doppler ultrasound and CT imaging.
